questions.question_text: Florida's Fair Housing Act covers which types of housing transactions?
questions.correct_answer: Sale, rental, and financing of dwellings
distractors: Only rental properties in the state
Only properties listed through a brokerage
questions.explanation: Florida's Fair Housing Act, like its federal counterpart, covers the sale, rental, and financing of residential dwellings. It protects against discrimination based on race, color, national origin, sex, disability, familial status, and religion.

questions.question_text: Which state agency administers the Florida Fair Housing Act?
distractors: Department of Business and Professional Regulation
questions.explanation: The Florida Commission on Human Relations (FCHR) administers the Florida Fair Housing Act. FCHR investigates complaints and attempts conciliation before recommending further action.

questions.question_text: A person believes they experienced housing discrimination in Florida. Within what time period must they file a complaint with FCHR?
questions.explanation: A complaint must be filed with FCHR within 1 year of the alleged discriminatory act. This is different from the federal HUD complaint deadline of 1 year.

questions.question_text: Compared to federal fair housing law, Florida's Fair Housing Act adds which additional protected classes?
questions.explanation: Florida adds no additional protected classes beyond federal law. The protected classes are: race, color, national origin, sex, disability, familial status, and religion — the same seven as federal law.

questions.question_text: After FCHR investigates a fair housing complaint and conciliation fails, the complainant may take what action?
questions.correct_answer: File a civil action in court
questions.explanation: If FCHR investigation and conciliation do not resolve the complaint, the complainant may file a civil action in court. Fair housing violations are civil matters, not criminal.

questions.question_text: Under Florida law, a landlord must notify a tenant of the security deposit holding method within how many days of receipt?
questions.explanation: The landlord must notify the tenant in writing within 30 days of receiving the deposit, stating the name and address of the bank and whether the account is interest-bearing or non-interest-bearing.

questions.question_text: A Florida landlord holds a security deposit in an interest-bearing account. What interest is owed to the tenant?
distractors: 100% of all earned interest on the deposit
No interest is owed to the tenant at all
questions.explanation: When held in an interest-bearing account, the landlord must pay the tenant either 75% of the annualized average interest rate or 5% simple interest per year, whichever the landlord chooses.

questions.question_text: A tenant moves out and the landlord has no claim against the deposit. By when must the landlord return it?
questions.explanation: If the landlord has no claim against the deposit, it must be returned within 15 days. If the landlord intends to make a claim, written notice must be sent by certified mail within 30 days.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a valid method for a Florida landlord to hold a security deposit?
questions.correct_answer: A personal savings account in the landlord's name
distractors: A surety bond with the clerk of court
questions.explanation: Florida law provides three specific options: non-interest-bearing account, interest-bearing account, or surety bond. A personal savings account would constitute commingling with the landlord's personal funds.

questions.question_text: A landlord sends a claim notice against the security deposit. The tenant has how many days to object?
questions.explanation: After the landlord sends a written claim notice by certified mail within 30 days, the tenant has 15 days to object. If the tenant does not object within 15 days, the landlord may deduct from the deposit.

questions.question_text: Under Florida law, a month-to-month tenancy requires how many days' notice to terminate?
questions.explanation: Florida requires 15 days' notice to terminate a month-to-month tenancy. This is shorter than many other states that require 30 days. A week-to-week tenancy requires 7 days' notice.

questions.question_text: A Florida tenant fails to pay rent. The landlord must provide what type of notice before filing for eviction?
questions.explanation: For nonpayment of rent, the landlord must give a 3-day notice (excluding weekends and legal holidays). This is different from the 7-day notice required for curable lease violations.

questions.question_text: A Florida landlord must give how much advance notice before entering a tenant's unit for a routine inspection?
questions.explanation: Florida law requires 12 hours' reasonable notice before a landlord can enter for inspections, repairs, or showings. The landlord may enter without notice only in case of emergency.

questions.question_text: A tenant violates a lease term that can be corrected. Under Florida law, the landlord must provide what notice?
questions.explanation: For curable lease violations, the landlord must give a 7-day notice allowing the tenant to correct the problem. For non-curable or repeated violations, a 7-day unconditional quit notice applies.

questions.question_text: A year-to-year tenancy in Florida requires how many days' notice before termination?
questions.correct_answer: 60 days before end of the annual period
distractors: 30 days before end of the annual period
90 days before end of the annual period
15 days before end of the annual period
questions.explanation: Year-to-year tenancies require 60 days' notice before the end of the annual period. Compare with month-to-month (15 days), quarterly (15 days before end of quarter), and week-to-week (7 days).
